metadata.title: Web App Persistence Solution Selector
metadata.description: A decision-tree questionnaire to help identify the most appropriate persistence strategy for your web application, replacing the current approach of writing JSON files into a directory.
metadata.author: Questionnaire TUI

questions.text: Where will the web application be deployed?
questions.description: The deployment environment constrains which persistence backends are available and practical.

options.label: Local / developer machine only
options.description: Runs on a single developer's machine, no production deployment planned

options.label: Single server (VPS / bare metal)
options.description: One persistent server with full filesystem access

options.label: Platform-as-a-Service (Heroku, Render, Railway, Fly.io)
options.description: Managed hosting platform; ephemeral or limited filesystem

options.label: Serverless / edge functions (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Workers)
options.description: Stateless compute; no persistent filesystem between invocations

options.label: Container orchestration (Kubernetes, ECS)
options.description: Containers may be rescheduled; persistent volumes require explicit setup

options.label: Cloud IaaS (EC2, GCE, Azure VM) with managed services available
options.description: Full cloud flexibility; managed databases available

questions.text: What are the biggest pain points with the current file-based JSON approach?
questions.description: Select all that apply. This helps prioritise which limitations must be solved.

options.label: Concurrent write conflicts (multiple users writing simultaneously)
options.description: Race conditions when two requests write the same file

options.label: No query capability (must load all files to search)
options.description: Cannot efficiently filter or aggregate responses

options.label: Poor scalability (many files slow down directory listing)
options.description: Performance degrades as the number of stored documents grows

options.label: No atomic transactions (partial writes leave corrupt state)
options.description: Crash mid-write can produce invalid data

options.label: Cannot share storage across multiple app instances
options.description: Horizontal scaling is blocked by local filesystem dependency

options.label: Difficult backup and restore
options.description: No built-in versioning, point-in-time recovery, or replication

options.label: Filesystem is ephemeral in the target deployment environment
options.description: Data is lost on process restart or container replacement

options.label: No access control on stored data
options.description: All server processes can read every user's responses

questions.text: How many concurrent users do you expect the application to serve?
questions.description: Concurrency requirements drive the need for a proper database engine.

options.label: 1 (personal tool, single operator)
options.description: Only one person will use the app at a time

options.label: 2–20 concurrent users
options.description: Small team or limited audience

options.label: 21–500 concurrent users
options.description: Medium-scale application

options.label: 500+ concurrent users
options.description: High-traffic production service

questions.text: How complex are the relationships between the data entities (questionnaires, responses, users)?
questions.description: Relational complexity favours SQL; document-centric data fits NoSQL well.

options.label: Flat / no relationships (each questionnaire response is self-contained)
options.description: Documents are independent; no joins needed

options.label: Simple relationships (responses linked to questionnaires and users)
options.description: A handful of foreign-key style links

options.label: Moderate relationships (users, sessions, questionnaires, responses, analytics)
options.description: Several entity types with references between them

options.label: Complex relationships (branching logic history, audit trails, role-based access)
options.description: Rich domain model requiring joins, aggregations, and integrity constraints

questions.text: Which query capabilities do you need?
questions.description: Select all that your application will require.

options.label: Fetch a single document by ID
options.description: Simple key-value lookup

options.label: List all records for a user or questionnaire
options.description: Basic enumeration without filtering

options.label: Filter by field value (status, date range, tag)
options.description: WHERE-clause style queries

options.label: Full-text search across response content
options.description: Search inside free-text answers

options.label: Aggregations and analytics (counts, averages, histograms)
options.description: Statistical summaries across many responses

options.label: Sorting and pagination
options.description: Ordered result sets with offset or cursor pagination

questions.text: How important are ACID guarantees (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) for your data?
questions.description: Financial data or regulated industries typically require strong ACID guarantees.

options.label: Not important — occasional data loss or inconsistency is acceptable
options.description: Informal surveys where losing a response has no consequence

options.label: Eventual consistency is acceptable — data must not be permanently lost
options.description: Temporary inconsistency is fine; durability is required

options.label: Strong consistency required — partial writes must never be visible
options.description: Users must see correct data immediately after a successful write

options.label: Full ACID transactions required — multi-step operations must be atomic
options.description: Financial, healthcare, or compliance-sensitive workflows

questions.text: Which data storage technologies does your team have hands-on experience with?
questions.description: Familiarity reduces operational risk and time-to-value.

options.label: Filesystem / local file storage (current approach)
options.description: Reading and writing files from Node.js

options.label: SQLite
options.description: Embedded relational database, no separate server

options.label: PostgreSQL
options.description: Full-featured open-source relational database

options.label: MySQL / MariaDB
options.description: Widely-used relational database

options.label: MongoDB
options.description: Document-oriented NoSQL database

options.label: DynamoDB
options.description: AWS managed NoSQL key-value and document store

options.label: Redis
options.description: In-memory data structure store, used for caching and sessions

options.label: Object storage (AWS S3, R2, MinIO)
options.description: Blob / object store; good for large files and JSON documents

options.label: None of the above — willing to learn
options.description: No prior experience; open to any recommendation

questions.text: Does the application need to run as multiple instances simultaneously (horizontal scaling)?
questions.description: If yes, local filesystem storage is not viable and a shared backend is required.

questions.text: Does the application need to work offline or with intermittent connectivity?
questions.description: Offline-capable apps may need client-side storage (IndexedDB, SQLite WASM) with sync.

questions.text: Does the application require real-time data updates pushed to clients (e.g., live results dashboard)?
questions.description: Real-time push typically requires a database with change streams or pub/sub (PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY, MongoDB Change Streams, Redis Pub/Sub).

questions.text: How many questionnaire responses do you expect to store in total over the lifetime of the application?
questions.description: Data volume affects storage cost, indexing strategy, and whether an embedded database suffices.

options.label: Fewer than 1,000 responses
options.description: Very small dataset; even flat files can work

options.label: 1,000 – 100,000 responses
options.description: Small-to-medium dataset; SQLite or a small hosted DB is appropriate

options.label: 100,000 – 10 million responses
options.description: Medium dataset; requires a proper database with good indexing

options.label: More than 10 million responses
options.description: Large scale; requires sharding, partitioning, or a managed cloud database

questions.text: What is your budget for managed database infrastructure per month?
questions.description: Cost influences whether a free embedded database, a managed cloud DB, or self-hosted solution is feasible.

options.label: Zero (free tier or self-hosted only)
options.description: No budget for paid managed services

options.label: Up to $50 / month
options.description: Entry-level managed tiers (Supabase free/pro, PlanetScale hobby, Neon free)

options.label: $50 – $500 / month
options.description: Standard managed DB plans suitable for production workloads

options.label: More than $500 / month
options.description: Enterprise-grade managed databases with high availability and SLA

questions.text: Are there regulatory or compliance requirements that apply to the stored data?
questions.description: Select all that apply. Compliance can restrict where and how data may be stored.

options.label: None
options.description: No specific regulatory requirements

options.label: GDPR (EU data protection)
options.description: Data must be stored in the EU or in adequate third countries; right to erasure applies

options.label: HIPAA (US healthcare)
options.description: Requires encryption at rest and in transit, audit logs, BAA agreements

options.label: PCI-DSS (payment card data)
options.description: Strict controls on cardholder data storage

options.label: Data residency (data must stay in a specific country or region)
options.description: Some jurisdictions mandate data not leave their borders

questions.text: How much tolerance does the project have for migrating existing JSON file data to the new system?
questions.description: Some options require a one-time or ongoing migration of existing stored responses.

options.label: Greenfield — no existing data to migrate
options.description: Starting fresh; no migration needed

options.label: Migration is acceptable with a one-time script
options.description: Willing to write and run a migration script once

options.label: Migration is risky — existing data must not be lost
options.description: Old data is business-critical; migration must be carefully validated

options.label: Prefer running old and new storage side-by-side during transition
options.description: Dual-write or shadow-read strategy to de-risk cutover

questions.text: How stable is the data schema (questionnaire and response structure)?
questions.description: Rapidly evolving schemas favour schema-less stores; stable schemas benefit from enforced schemas in SQL.

options.label: Stable — schema is unlikely to change significantly
options.description: Well-defined, versioned structure; SQL is a good fit

options.label: Evolving — new question types and fields will be added regularly
options.description: Frequent schema changes; JSON column in SQL or document DB helps

options.label: Highly dynamic — structure varies widely between questionnaires
options.description: Schema-less storage (document DB, JSON columns, object store) preferred

questions.text: How much operational complexity can your team manage?
questions.description: 1 = Minimal (prefer fully managed, zero-ops solutions), 10 = High (comfortable self-hosting, tuning, and managing infrastructure)

questions.text: Do you already have a preferred persistence solution in mind?
questions.description: If you have a strong preference, this questionnaire can validate whether it fits your requirements.

options.label: No preference — recommend the best fit
options.description: Fully open to the recommendation

options.label: SQLite (embedded, file-based relational DB)
options.description: Simple, zero-server, great for single-instance apps

options.label: PostgreSQL (self-hosted or managed)
options.description: Powerful open-source relational DB with JSONB support

options.label: MongoDB (document store)
options.description: Flexible schema, good for document-centric data

options.label: DynamoDB (AWS managed NoSQL)
options.description: Serverless-friendly, pay-per-use AWS service

options.label: Object storage (S3 / R2 / MinIO)
options.description: Keep JSON files but store them in an object store instead of disk

options.label: Supabase (managed PostgreSQL + Auth + Realtime)
options.description: Open-source Firebase alternative built on Postgres

options.label: Firebase / Firestore (Google managed)
options.description: Real-time document store with offline sync and auth

questions.text: Are there any other constraints, requirements, or context that should influence the recommendation?
questions.description: For example: existing infrastructure, vendor lock-in concerns, team size, time constraints, open-source requirements.
